EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ongoing dry phase of El Ni��o continues, especially for islands north of 5N across Micronesia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By the beginning of February, Majuro, Mili, and nearby islands degraded to D1 from D0, but passing trade-wind disturbances improved the islands back to D0 February 16th. The northern RMI continued to see below the 2 inches per week needed to keep water supplies healthy. Kwajalein, which was holding steady at D0 degraded to D1 on February 6th and then to D2 February 27th, while Wotje and nearby islands degraded to D2 on February 13th. Compared to January, when the quick onset of drought overcame local resources, local and national government of the RMI were overall handling the need for aid due to the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ongoing dry phase of El Ni��o continues, especially for islands north of 5N across Micronesia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yap Proper, Ulithi and other nearby islands of Yap State, degraded or remained in D2 (severe) drought through February, while Woleai went from D0 to D1. At this time water supplies were decreasing but the local state government was able to handle the need for aid during this time with the help of the FSM national government. In Chuuk State, most islands fluctuated between D0 or no drought due to periods of wet weather across the State, though Lukunor did briefly enter D1 around February 20th. Pohnpei State saw Pohnpei remain in D1 through most of the month before improving to D0 February 27, while Pingelap went from D1 to D2 February 13th. Similar to Yap, Chuuk and Pohnpei local governments was able to handle the need for aid during this time with the help of the FSM national government. This was an improvement from January, when the quick onset of drought overcame local resources.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Palau started to experience a period of drier weather in March that was not meeting the 2 inch per week to meet water needs as the dry phase of El Ni��o continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On March 26th the National Emergency Committee declared a Water Shortage Watch for the republic as water levels at Ngerimel and Ngerikiil reservoirs, Airai, have significantly dropped due to insufficient rainfall and heightened water consumption. This placed the Republic of Palau under Stage 1 of the Drought Action Plan, encouraging residents to practice water conservation. Palau on the Drought Monitor was in D0 (no drought or dryness), but did increase to D0 (abnormally dry).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distant but powerful extratropical cyclone passing well north of Micronesia, created a long period north swell that traveled through the region. It generated very high surf and led to quick and extensive coastal flooding within the Republic of the Marshall islands and parts of Micronesia. The swell also moved into the southern hemisphere with some impacts being reported across the Samoan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From Kosrae State, several videos were shared on social media on January 21st, showing coastal flooding, likely from the same large swell event that affected the Marshall Islands and Wake Island on January 19th to 21st (See coastal flood for Wake Island and Marshall Islands). Inundation occurred mainly on the northern shores of Kosrae, pushing debris over roads and into yards and homes. Info forwarded by the Micronesia Red Cross Kosrae chapter (part of International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies), reported that flooding/waves damaged the main water line and people of Walung were out of water. Also, most people near the shore were displaced and took temporary shelter at the Walung Elementary School. As of the end of March 2024, no injuries were reported from this event. Some coastal flooding was also reported on Lukunoch Island, in Chuuk State (see the other Micronesia coastal flood event), and some minor coastal flooding even at Kapingamarangi in southern Pohnpei State, but very few impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large north swell generated by a distant extratropical cyclone, along with elevated wind waves and northeast swell from a descending shear line into the Marianas, created dangerous surf and rip currents along west and north facing reefs of the Marianas from January 17-19, with surf then slowly decreasing through January 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighters were called to a beach near the village of Asan (across from the old Harley-Davidson store) for a report of two swimmers in distress at 1424ChST. A 13-year-old boy was found and pulled onto the boat, and he did not require any medical attention. the 8-year-old was later found and CPR administered as he was transported to Naval Hospital. It was later reported that the 8-year-old boy later passed away at the hospital. This occurred at the tail end of large surf event created by large north swell and a shear line that brought choppy seas due to strong winds, that started January 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distant but powerful extratropical cyclone passing well north of Micronesia, created a long period north swell that traveled through the region. It generated very high surf and led to quick and extensive coastal flooding within the Republic of the Marshall islands and parts of Micronesia. The swell also moved into the southern hemisphere with some impacts being reported across the Samoan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A synoptic observer that sends data to the Weather Service Office in Chuuk, reported coastal flooding on Lukunoch Islands on January 22nd from 0900 ChST to 1500 ChST. Flooding came from the northeast, likely from the same large swell event that affected the Marshall Islands and Wake Island on January 19th to 21st (See coastal flood for Wake Island and Marshall Islands). Salt water flooded into some taro patches on the island, causing the community to dig some drainage ditches to help rid the salt water to help limit crop damage. A local mayor evacuated  people from six houses that were severely damaged from the flooding to a village located further south on the island. No injuries were reported. Based on reports from the island, a rough estimate for crop damage was about one thousand USD, with total property damage to the six houses starting around fifty thousand USD. No official damage reports have been received at WFO Guam since the initial report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distant but powerful extratropical cyclone passing well north of Micronesia, created a long period north swell that traveled through the region. It generated very high surf and led to quick and extensive coastal flooding within the Republic of the Marshall islands and parts of Micronesia. The swell also moved into the southern hemisphere with some impacts being reported across the Samoan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The north shores began seeing higher than normal water levels on January 18th, with flooding occurring between 2200LST (2000ChST) on January 19th through 0900LST (0700 ChST) on January 20th. The Downtown area, located on the north side of the lagoon of Wake island, was flooded by water from the north reefs/shores flooding all the barracks and dining facilities. All of the barrack were made off limit and considered unsafe, and everyone affected was moved into MDA, manager and DET (Commander's) housing. Water then went across the lagoon, flooding the taxiway and washed out the dirt road to Wilkes Islands. No injuries were reported for this event. The tide station on Wake Island is located on the southern side of the atoll and was sheltered for the event. However, the tide gage did report water levels above tide prediction, with water levels exceeding predictions by about 0.6 feet from both MSL and MLLW. As of end of March 2024, no damages estimates have been made public for Wake Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distant but powerful extratropical cyclone passing well north of Micronesia, created a long period north swell that traveled through the region. It generated very high surf and led to quick and extensive coastal flooding within the Republic of the Marshall islands and parts of Micronesia. The swell also moved into the southern hemisphere with some impacts being reported across the Samoan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roi-Namur, one of the islands within the Kwajalein Atoll, experience very large surf, with some witness reports claiming waves exceeding 20 feet, with videos on land showing water of 3 to 4 feet hitting and washing through the Army's dining hall on Roi-Namur. The event was described as a series of 4-6 waves that occurred over the course of 30-45 minutes, that occurred between 2020-2100LST (1820-1900 ChST) on January 20th, with waters quickly receding after the last large wave. Eighty people were evacuated to Kwajalein Island, with one person treated for injuries at the clinic on Kwajalein Island and no deaths reported. Aerial damage assessment conducted by the Army showed that inundation washed over the northwest side of the island, flooding at least one-third of it, with standing water on both sides of the north end of the runway and the first floors of all but two bachelors' quarters.  No subsequent inundation episodes were reported on Roi, even during high tide at 0052LST and 1342LST on January 21st. Other Islands within the Marshall Islands reported some moderate inundation such as Woja in Ailinglaplap Atoll, were some erosion stretch from the shore line to the airfield, with debris was also tossed onto airfield. Minor inundation was reported at Arno atoll (located just east of Majuro) and Kili Island, home of the displaced Bikini Islanders. Damage estimates are still ongoing, but the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) had an initial estimate of 55 Thousand USD at the end of January, but total damage cost may end up being much higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: El Ni��o developed during the summer of 2023, but the western Pacific experienced a below average year in Tropical Cyclone (TC) activity with only 19 TCs developing. This, along with a quick return of a dry trade-wind pattern in December of 2023, led to below average rainfall from much of Micronesia. This drier pattern continue through January as the dry phase of El Ni��o began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ulithi, an island in Yap State and located just northeast of Yap Proper, entered Severe Drought (D2) as December of 2023 ended with 3.87 inches of rain which is ranked the seventh driest in a 41 year period, with eighth consecutive weeks below rainfall for water needs. Yap entered moderate drought (D1) with 3.37 inches of rain which is ranked third driest out of 73 year period, but only five consecutive weeks below rainfall for water needs. Within Chuuk State, Lukunor atoll entered D0 (Abnormally Dry), along with the following islands in Pohnpei State: Pohnpie, Pingelap, and Nukuoro. On January 10th, the Governor of Pohnpei State declared a state of emergency for the main island of Pohnpei and the outer islands of the State. Pohnpei State had been experiencing shortage of waters, with schools, hospitals and dispensaries to be prioritized for water availability, with schools being on half day schedules for students. The Drought Monitor updated the main island of Pohnpei to D1 (Moderate Drought) January 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: El Ni��o developed during the summer of 2023, but the western Pacific experienced a below average year in Tropical Cyclone (TC) activity with only 19 TCs developing. This, along with a quick return of a dry trade-wind pattern in December of 2023, led to below average rainfall from much of Micronesia. This drier pattern continue through January as the dry phase of El Ni��o began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions quickly spread across the Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI), following a dry December. The Drought Monitor at beginning of January had Wotje, one of the northern atolls of the RMI in D1 (Moderate Drought), while no other island were labeled based on satellite rainfall estimates and rain gauge data. However, reports from the outer islands of the RMI started to come and were forwarded by the Weather Service Office (WSO) in Majuro.  On January 9th, The Ministry of Education of the RMI reported water scarcity at the Jaluit High School (JHS) in Jaluit Atoll, stating rain had not fallen on Jaluit in a month. For hygiene purposes JHS students and staff had been getting water from the community and the old WWII water catchment located on the ocean side of the school. The drinking water catchments were dry and JHS was relying on Japan-donated water storage unit for drinking and cooking. The Reverse Osmosis machine for JHS was non-operational and it was predicted the school would run out of water for all purposes around January 12th. On January 17th (local time) the Drought Monitor had Majuro, Kwajalein, Jaluit, and Mili in D0 (Abnormally Dry). Majuro was upgraded to D1 January 24th, and on January 30th Mili was also added to D1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the dry phase of the El Ni��o continues, drought worsens in parts of Micronesia and spreads to the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wotje and nearby islands of the northern RMI degraded from D2 (severe) to D3 (extreme), and Majuro degraded from D1 (moderate) to D2 on the March 26th Drought Monitor. Kwajalein, which was in D2, improved to D1 on March 19th.  Main impacts were water shortages for Wotje and nearby islands the northern RMI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the dry phase of the El Ni��o continues, drought worsens in parts of Micronesia and spreads to the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ulithi and other nearby islands of Yap State, remained in D2 (severe) through March, while Woleai remained in D1. Yap Proper degraded to D3 (extreme) on March 26th. Yap State Government scheduled a ship to depart April 6th to deliver 90 pallets of water to the outer islands. Flights conducted by Pacific Mission Aviation, delivered 250 gallons of bottles of water to Ulithi and nearby Fais on March 27th. Ulithi was also reporting island water tank was now half full with crops turing yellow. Woleai Island's water take was very low and crop there were also turning yellow. In Chuuk State, most islands fluctuated between D0 or no drought due to periods of wet weather across the State. Pohnpei State, on March 19th, saw Pohnpei improve to no dryness on March 19th, with Pingelap improving from D2 to D1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the dry phase of the El Ni��o continues, drought worsens in parts of Micronesia and spreads to the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saipan and Tinian entered D2 (severe) drought as weekly minimum of 1 inch of rain were not being met. No water or major agricultural impacts reported, but saw an increase in wildfires reported on the islands as vegetation continued to dry out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the dry phase of the El Ni��o continues, drought worsens in parts of Micronesia and spreads to the Marianas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam degraded to D2 (severe) drought as weekly minimum of 1 inch of rain were not being met. The reports of small wildfires continued to increase since February as sword grass and other vegetation continued to dry out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trade-wind surge from Hawaii to the Marshall Islands generated a large easterly trade surge, while a strong extratropical system moving into the Pacific from Japan generated a elevated north swell into the region. This event coinciding with the King Tide Cycle around March 10th, where high tides were higher than normal, leading to coastal flooding, especially in the Marshall Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Substantial inundation was already occurring on March 9th along the southeast-facing gravel road next to the Kwajalein Country Club extending SW toward the weather station, shortly after peak tide of 5.1 ft at 1359ChST (1559LST). A pool had formed on the golf course greens just southwest of the Country Club as well. Inundation reportedly worsened on 10th and 11th of March. March 10th was the expected peak in King tides and trade swells for Kwajalein. A roughly 1/8 mi stretch of east-facing gravel road along the North Point of Kwajalein Island was at least partially inundated by ankle deep water, covered in rocks, coconuts, fronds, and coral debris. Water levels came up close to one or two resident's yards, but no homes on Kwajalein reported any inundation. The evening of 12 March, employee of the RTS Weather Station, saw little standing water left, but still plenty of debris. Debris from wash-over was evident in a few spots along the north-facing road on Kwajalein Island's North Point as well, showing that some waves had penetrated well into the shallows past the reef.|Ebeye Island - located north of Kwajalein Island in the Kwajalein Atoll, reported flooding along mainly the northeast side of the island - lots of debris. The Gugeegue Causeway was closed and impassable, and school was cancelled as a result.|Roi-Namur - Located on the northern tip of Kwajalein atoll, reported flooding and debris reported mainly along the east-facing road adjacent to the runway. Flooding likely impacted other north- and east-facing shorelines.|Jaluit Atoll - On March 10th, around 1500ChST (1700ChST), an inspection team from the Minister of Public Works, Infrastructure and Utilities, responded to reports of inundation on Jabor Island. Waves reportedly came over the seawall, and came in through the boy's dormitory at one of the schools, causing some damage to infrastructure. Water also came up to other nearby homes an a hotel along the main road. The road between Jabor and the airport terminal was also covered in rocks from the inundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-period north swell generated by a distant low pressure system off the coast of Japan generated large surf along west, north, and east facing reefs and caused minor coastal inundation along some north facing shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf generated by long-period north swell, generating high surf estimated to be exceeding 12 feet. This pushed water and debris up to the shelters at the Paseo in Hagatna, and the picnic areas and parking lot at Asan beach. A NWS employee at the Paseo sent photos around 1221ChST showing water ponding of a couple of inches (less than 1/2 foot) around the shelters about 25 to 30 yards from the water's edge. Around 1340ChST, a second NWS employee called from Asan Beach Park located about 3 miles south of the first location, stating significant amount of debris across the parking lot and waves reaching the picnic area. Some of the parking lot are as close about 10 yards from the water's edge during high tide, but most of the picnic area and parking lots are about 20 to 30 yards from the waters edge around high tide. High tide on March 1st occurred around 1038ChST, according to the tide station at Apra Harbor. Predicted height, based off of MLLW, was 2.04 ft, with actual water level measured at 2.66 ft. Tide around the time of the first report at 1221ChST was predicted to be around 1.174 ft and the actual water level recorded was 2.26 ft. Around 1600ChST the office stopped receiving reports of coastal inundation, likely due to the approaching low tide at 1734ChST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakening northeast winds and mostly clear skies, ahead of a shear line near Alamagan in the far northern Mariana Islands, created conditions just right for a rare dust devil during the morning of March 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of the Dust Devil was caught on a backyard security camera and was provide to WFO Guam. A team from WFO Guam also went to the property and interviewed nearby residents Primary damage found was to a couple of canopies that was dead-in street between two homes. Some of the legs of the both canopy were tied to cinder blocks, but part of one the canopy's metal frames ended up being tossed on top of the one story house is was next to. Total property damage was estimated to be around $200 USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of northerly and easterly swells led to hazardous conditions in the surf zone of Guam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam Fire Department received a call at 1100ChST of distressed swimmers at Ritidian Beach. GFD Rescue, Coast Guard and the Navy's HSC-25 helicopter squadron were on scene to conduct search and rescue operations. When GFD arrived, they learned a 28-year-old man was last seen going over the reef, while two other men who were also with the first swimmer were able to make it back to shore. One of the two men who made it back to shore was transported to Guam Regional Medial City for evaluation, while the other refused any service. The body of the man who went over the reef, was found approximately 25 fee below the surface, at 0809ChST the following day. Ritidian Beach is known for its strong rip currents as the reef line is relatively close to shore and there are large cuts in the reef that favor rip currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of northerly and easterly swells led to hazardous conditions in the surf zone of Guam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1328ChST, Guam Fire Department (GFD) received a report that a 40-year-old fisherman was overdue. He was fishing in the waters off Asan Beach. The search was called off Wednesday night, but was resumed 0600ChST Thursday morning. At 0752ChST Thursday morning, GFD divers located the fisherman's body at approximately 45 feet below the surface of the water. It is  believed the fisherman was wading in the water and got caught in a rip current that pulled in deeper into the water and towards the reef line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Placeholder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam Forestry Division and fire departments fought a number of fires across Malesso', Humatak and Hagat, located across southern Guam. The largest fire was in Malesso' where and estimated 130-153 acres of a combination of grass and forest land were burned by a fire intentionally started. The Malesso' fire burned through a reforestation project maintained by Guam Forestry off Quinene Road. The fire did not burn the whole forest, roughly 21 acres in size, and some of the older trees are expected to recover. The percentage of recovery still has yet to be determined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palau had improved from abnormally dry to to no drought or dryness at the end of April, but remained under Stage 1 of the National Drought Action Plan since March 26th. On May 14 at 1030LST (1230ChST), the National Emergency Management Office (NEMO) of the Republic of Palau issued a updated message stating the Stage 1 Declaration was rescinded due to water levels rising and remaining above the 85% threshold, with long-term weather outlooks showing a decreasing risk of long-term dryness and drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI) south of 9N continued to see improving drought condition through May with most islands improving from moderate (D1) drought to abnormally dry (D0) or no drought or dryness. This resulted from the strong winter subtropical ridge starting to weaken and lift north, allowing trade-wind disturbance that have been mainly near the equator to lift northward into the Marshall Islands. This pattern also started to bring drought relief to Wotje and nearby islands in the northern RMI, was in exceptional (D4) drought at the end of April. On May 7th, Wotje had improved to extreme (D3) drought, and this trend continued as Wotje further improved to severe (D2) drought on May 21st, and finally improved to moderate (D1) drought on May 28th. For the month of May, 6.94 inches of rain fell at Wotje, up from the 3.32 inches recorded in April, and the 0.00 inches reported in March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saipan and Tinian remained steady in extreme (D3) drought for the month of May as both islands did not meet 1 inch weekly minimum rainfall requirements to meet water needs. Saipan International airport ended May with 1.26 inches of rain, while Tinian airport ended the month with 0.85 inches. Vegetation remained yellowed and dry across the island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yap State remains the hardest hit by short-term and long-term drought in the FSM. Yap Proper, Ulithi and nearby islands were in exceptional (D4) drought at the start of May. These islands remained in D4 until May 28th, when they improved slightly to extreme (D3) drought in response to heavy showers associated with Invest 93W around May 22nd, with Yap Proper reporting 0.93 inches and Ulithi reporting 2.70 inches, before this disturbance would develop further into 01W (Ewiniar) in the Philippine Seas.  Woleai, an island in eastern Yap State, entered May in severe (D2) drought, and would fluctuate D2 and moderate (D1) drought as passing trade-wind disturbances, including 93W, brought periods of increased rainfall to Woleai and nearby islands. Woleai then remained in D2 from May 21st to the end of May as a dry pattern established itself over Yap State near the end of May. Reports from Yap State mentioned minor improvements in water levels in water catchments and other water storage systems, with 3 of the 4 water authorities on Yap Proper, reporting water supplies starting to rise after experiencing months of water levels dropping. However, short-term and long-term drought impacts continued to strain water and agricultural resources, with residents continuing to be urged to ration fresh water usage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam held steady at severe (D2) drought as most weeks failed to meet the 1 inch weekly minimum requirements to meet water needs and Guam ended the month with 3.82 inches at the weather service office. Vegetation started to yellow again, after briefly greening at the beginning of the month from the heavy rain fall back on April 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the now waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State and the Mariana Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After holding steady at moderate (D1) drought since February, Rota degraded to severe (D2) drought and further degraded to extreme drought (D3) on May 14th. Rota by May 7th was about 50 percent of normal rainfall for the calendar year to date. At the end of the May, 2.84 inches of rain was reported at the Rota Airport for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased rainfall across Micronesia led to improving condition across the region, taking Yap and the Marshall Islands out of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yap Proper, Ulithi, and nearby islands started the month of July in D2 (severe) drought. As the summer monsoon pattern continued to develop across western Micronesia, islands started to get consecutive weeks of over 1 inch of rainfall. This allowed Ulithi and nearby islands to improve to D1 (moderate) on the release of the July 2nd drought monitor, with Yap Proper following July 16th as conditions continued to improve across all of Yap State. Ulithi ended July with 12.64 inches of rain while the weather service office on Yap Proper ended July with 20.23 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased rainfall across Micronesia led to improving condition across the region, taking Yap and the Marshall Islands out of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wotje, one of the northern islands of the Marshall islands was the only monitored island with observations that was in D2 (severe) drought at the beginning of July. Due to the lack of rain reported from Wotje, it was degraded to D3 (extreme drought) on July 16th. The drought monitor would keep it at this category through July. However, near the end of July questions started to be raised of possible exposer issues of the rain gage as rain amounts did not appear to be matching what was expected to satellite imagery and reports from relatively nearby islands such as Kwajalein. The weather service office on Majuro later confirmed there were exposure issues at Wotje and that no drought impacts were being reported or observed. With the exclusion of Wotje, the U.S. Drought Monitor indicated that the Republic of the Marshall Islands had no drought or dryness at the end of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State. In June, CPC announced that El Ni��o and transitioned to  ENSO-neutral. Rainfall increased across the Marianas as the region entered a more typical summer pattern with the Tropical Upper Tropospheric Trough (TUTT) extending across the central and west Pacific.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rota entered June at extreme (D3) drought, but increased rainfall improved drought conditions becoming severe (D2) June 18th and further improving to moderate (D1) drought June 25th. Rota Airport ended the month with 6.79 inches, exceeding the 1 inch minimum weekly requirement to meet water needs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State. In June, CPC announced that El Ni��o and transitioned to  ENSO-neutral. Rainfall increased across the Marianas as the region entered a more typical summer pattern with the Tropical Upper Tropospheric Trough (TUTT) extending across the central and west Pacific.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yap State remains the hardest hit by short-term and long-term drought in the FSM. Yap Proper, Ulithi and nearby islands started June in extreme (D3) drought. Photos taken June 4th of the Gitam Reservoir on Yap Proper, showed water levels at the reservoir are near or below levels recorded during the 2016 drought. These islands remained in D3 until June 25th, when they improved to severe (D2) drought in as the slow transition to the summer monsoon pattern started to bring increased rainfall western Micronesia. Woleai, an island in eastern Yap State, entered June in severe (D2) drought, and would improve to (D1) drought June 4th as passing trade-wind disturbances brought periods of increased rainfall to Woleai and nearby islands. Woleai then remained in D1 for the month of June. Overall, short-term and long-term drought impacts continued to strain water and agricultural resources across Yap State, with residents continuing to be urged to ration fresh water usage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State. In June, CPC announced that El Ni��o and transitioned to  ENSO-neutral. Rainfall increased across the Marianas as the region entered a more typical summer pattern with the Tropical Upper Tropospheric Trough (TUTT) extending across the central and west Pacific.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saipan and Tinian started June in extreme (D3) drought and improved to severe (D2) drought June 11th, and further improving to moderate (D1) June 18th and then abnormally dry (D0) June 25th.  Both islands met or exceeded the 1 inch weekly minimum rainfall t meet water needs. Saipan International airport ended JUne with 7.71 inches of rain, while Tinian airport ended the month with 6.96 inches. Vegetation also became greener, after yellowing in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Greatest impacts from the drought created by the dry season and dry phase of the waning El Ni��o continued to be the greatest across islands near and north of 8N, especially across Yap State. In June, CPC announced that El Ni��o and transitioned to  ENSO-neutral. Rainfall increased across the Marianas as the region entered a more typical summer pattern with the Tropical Upper Tropospheric Trough (TUTT) extending across the central and west Pacific.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam started June at severe (D2) drought, improving to moderate (D1) drought June 11th, and improving further to abnormally dry (D0) June 18th. Guam exceeded the 1 inch weekly minimum requirements to meet water needs, ending the month with 8.00 inches at the National Weather Service office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active trade-wind and TUTT pattern brought increased rainfall to most of the Republic of the Marshall islands (RMI), however rainfall reports from Wotje, one the northern islands in the RMI indicated a period of dry weather had returned. Given the dry conditions reported earlier in the year, concern for drought impacts returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI) south of 9N continued to see little to no drought or dryness. For the northern RMI however, rainfall observations from Wotje, which ended May with 6.94 inches of rain, reported several days without rain and ended June with 2.24 inches. Wotje, which saw improvement to  moderate (D1) drought at the end of may, degraded to D2 June 18th and remained at D2 for the rest of the month. Wojte had reported below average rainfall for most of the year so far, causing the report of decreased rainfall to spark concerns that drought impacts would return, leading to the degrading to D2 in the Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought impacts persisted across Yap State, the Marianas, and the Northern Marshall Islands as the dry season continued along with the dry phase of El Ni��o, especially for the islands near and north of 7N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On April 9th, Wotje and nearby islands of the northern RMI degraded from extreme (D3) to exceptional (D4) drought while Majuro degraded from severe (D2) to extreme (D3). Ailinglapalap also went from abnormally dry (D0) to moderate (D1) drought on April 9th, but quickly returned to abnormally dry on April 16th. Kwajalein remained in moderate drought (D1) through the whole month. Trade-wind disturbances started to increase across the central and southern islands of the RMI in the second half of April, causing Majuro to drop to from D3 to D2 April 23, and then to D1 April 30th. Wotje and nearby northern islands remained at D4 through the end of April. Main impacts continued to be the shortage of drinking water and the need of water deliveries for Wotje and nearby northern islands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought impacts persisted across Yap State, the Marianas, and the Northern Marshall Islands as the dry season continued along with the dry phase of El Ni��o, especially for the islands near and north of 7N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palau was abnormally dry (D0) at the start of April and eventually improved to no drought or dryness on April 23rd due to increasing showers activity. Palau was placed under Stage 1 of the National Drought Action Plan back on March 26th, encouraging water conservation, and this was continued for the entire month of April as water levels had yet to recover and maintain above the 85% threshold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought impacts persisted across Yap State, the Marianas, and the Northern Marshall Islands as the dry season continued along with the dry phase of El Ni��o, especially for the islands near and north of 7N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saipan and Tinian degraded from severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought on April 16th and remained at D3 through the month. From March through April, Saipan saw its peak in frequency of small wildfires as vegetation continued to dry and yellow across the island. No other major impacts were reported in response to the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought impacts persisted across Yap State, the Marianas, and the Northern Marshall Islands as the dry season continued along with the dry phase of El Ni��o, especially for the islands near and north of 7N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yap State continued to be the hardest impacted state within the Federated States of Micronesia (FSM) when it came to the drought across the region. Yap Proper was in extreme drought (D3) at the beginning of April and degraded to exceptional drought (D4) on April 9th. Nearby Ulithi and surrounding islands degraded from severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought on April 9th, latter degrading to exceptional drought (D4) on April 16th. Woleai, an island in eastern Yap State, was in moderate (D1) drought in the beginning April and degraded to D2 on April 9th, and then to D3 on April 16th before improving back to D2 on April 23rd as passing trade-wind disturbances brought increased rainfall to Woleai and nearby islands. The U.S. Coast Guard, with U.S. embassy and USAID partners continued to delivered more than 15,000 lbs of essential drought relief supplies, mainly to Yap State, from April 9-13th.  Across the rest of the FSM, drought conditions continued improved. Chuuk Lagoon in Chuuk State saw D1 conditions become D0 April 16th, while Pingelap in Pohnpei State, improved to D0 at the beginning of April and no drought or dryness reported April 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought impacts persisted across Yap State, the Marianas, and the Northern Marshall Islands as the dry season continued along with the dry phase of El Ni��o, especially for the islands near and north of 7N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam degraded from severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought on April 16th and remained at D3 until April 30th when it improved to D2 after a surface trough and upper-level feature dropped over 5.5 inches of rain on April 24th. Main impact was several small wildfires as vegetation continued to dry and yellow across the island. This included a small 5 acres fire on Cocos islands on April 2nd. Cocos Islands, which is a 95 acres island within the reef just south of Guam, is home to a population of endangered koko' birds and other birds and reptiles that are rare or no longer found on Guam.  No other major impacts were reported in response to the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Marianas was under a seasonal trade-wind pattern, with the the primary swell being the trade swell, so highest surf was along east facing reefs and lower along west facing reefs. Rip Currents were likely associated with cuts or channels within the reef, which are know to generate strong currents even when surf is not abnormally high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam Fire Department responded to two kayakers that were distressed at the Hag��t��a Boat Basin Channel around 0913ChST. one man was 55 years old and was unconscious and being assisted by a local surfer. CPR was started and he was transported to Naval Hospital. The second man was 61 years old and was transported to Guam Memorial Hospital with minor injuries. The final condition of the 55-year-old man is currently unknown to the NWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Marianas was under a seasonal trade-wind pattern, with the the primary swell being the trade swell, so highest surf was along east facing reefs and lower along west facing reefs. Rip Currents were likely associated with cuts or channels within the reef, which are know to generate strong currents even when surf is not abnormally high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam Fire Department (GFD) Fire and Water Rescue responded to a report of one distressed swimmer and one snorkeler who was unaccounted for at 1425ChST around First Beach of Ipan, Talo���fo���fo, located on the eastern side of Guam. When Guam Fire and Water Rescue arrived, a 27-year-old man was already brought into shore via bystanders. GFD personnel initiated CPR and the victim was transported to Naval Hospital, while the second victim, a 28-year-old man, was treated for minor injuries on shore and also transported to Naval Hospital. It is believed that the 27-year-old man passed due to drowning as WFO Guam was later contacted for weather and sea information for December 7th, regarding a drowning of a service member, which was near the location of where the 27 year-old-man was pulled from the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A kayak capsized off the east coast of Ngaraard, Palau near the village of Elab. Strong currents within Ngedub Channel pulled the fisherman out into the open ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to a news article in the Islands Times, a newspaper in Palau, two men from Ngaraard went fishing of the east side of island when their kayak overturned Some time after noon. One man, 32 years old, was swept out to sea, while the other, age not given, grabbed onto a buoy within the channel. The man who grabbed the buoy eventually decided not to fight the strong currents and drifted out of channel, but later was swept back over the reef and he was able to get back to shore early in the evening. The 32 year old man was never found and assumed lost. Altimetry and buoy data near Palau reported seas around 7 to 8 feet with a mixed east swell and secondary NNW swell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trade-wind surge east of the Marianas and near the northern Marshall Islands, generated a elevated trade swell that increased surf and the risk of rip currents along eastern shores of the Marianas. Some of the strongest rip currents typically occur along cuts or channels within the reef. This can create dangerous conditions for swimmers, or people on personal craft such as paddleboards and kayaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam fire Department (GFD) Fire and Water Rescue rescued two fishermen that were trapped in the Togcha Channel around 1930ChST, located behind Jeff's Priates Cove in the beach side area known as Ipan within the village of Talo���fo���fo. Both fishermen made it back to shore with the assistance of GFD personnel and refused all medical services.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winds associated with monsoon surge impacted the Marianas around September 16th as a monsoon depression (Invest 97W) developed and would later become Tropical Storm Pulasan in the Philippine Sea, west of the Marianas. Westerly swell from the overall monsoon pattern affected western Micronesia and the Marianas for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Starting around 1230ChST, observations started to show sustained winds around 35 knots (40 mph) and continued until about 2230ChST when sustained winds and gusts started to drop off overnight. A max gust of 51 knots (59 mph) was recorded at Andersen Air Force Base around 1341ChST. A number of departing and arriving flights were canceled or delayed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winds associated with monsoon surge impacted the Marianas around September 16th as a monsoon depression (Invest 97W) developed and would later become Tropical Storm Pulasan in the Philippine Sea, west of the Marianas. Westerly swell from the overall monsoon pattern affected western Micronesia and the Marianas for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the rain during the monsoon surge fell between 1100ChST and 1700ChST with most of the island receiving between 1.97 and 3.76 inches of rain during that time period.  The weather office recorded 3.76 inches had fallen from midnight to  1600ChST and ended the day at midnight with a 24 hour total of 5.62 inches. Newspapers reported that Polaris Point and other sections of Route 1 in Tamuning and Route 4 in Talofofo were reported to be impassable, while many roads across Guam were flooded but remained passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winds associated with monsoon surge impacted the Marianas around September 16th as a monsoon depression (Invest 97W) developed and would later become Tropical Storm Pulasan in the Philippine Sea, west of the Marianas. Westerly swell from the overall monsoon pattern affected western Micronesia and the Marianas for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For Saipan, wind started to gust over 30 kts after 0300ChST, and reach higher gusts than what was reported a weak earlier when TS Bebinca moved near the Marianas. Near Saipan's airport (215 ft in elevation), max sustained wind of 29 kt (33 mph) was recorded around 0855ChST with a max gust of 48 kt (55 mph) around 0905ChST. Higher elevations saw stronger winds with a private weather station on Wireless Ridge (elevation of 673 ft) reporting max sustained wind of 46 kt (53 mph) at 1355ChST and a max gust of 53 kt (61 mph) at 1305ChST. No notable damage was reported by the emergency management office up on Saipan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winds associated with monsoon surge impacted the Marianas around September 16th as a monsoon depression (Invest 97W) developed and would later become Tropical Storm Pulasan in the Philippine Sea, west of the Marianas. Westerly swell from the overall monsoon pattern affected western Micronesia and the Marianas for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest to west swell and wind waves associated with the monsoon surge, generated large surf along west facing reefs of the Marianas. at 1127ChST on September 17th, three distressed swimmers (2 children and 1 adult) were swept over the reef by large waves off of Gun Beach in Tumon. When Guam Fire Department and Water Rescue arrived, the first distressed swimmer, a 12-year-old boy, was already back on shore and suffered minor injures. The second swimmer rescued was a 47-year-old man, as rescued by boat and CPR was conducted, before being transported to a nearby hospital. The third simmer was a 10-year-old boy who was retrieved by the Navy's Helicopter Sea Combat Squadron (HSC) 25. CPR was also conducted and the boy was also transported to a hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Only minimal/weak damage reported across Guam with this very low-end tropical storm. Wind damage was limited to anecdotal reports of a couple banana trees, loose/dead limbs, and weak political campaign signs. Andersen Air Force Base, located on northern Guam, reporting the highest winds of the event with max sustained winds around 36 mph around 0235ChST and a peak gust around 59 mph at 0221ChST. Spotty power outages occurred throughout the passage, but outages were brief. Flooding occurred in the usual places during heavy rainfall: Polaris Point/Route 1 in Sumay-Piti corridor and other common urban flood areas. Most of Guam saw 24 hour rainfall (2200ChST on September 10th to 2200ChST on September 11th) of between 5 and 7.6 inches. There were no responses/requests for assistance by the American Red Cross. 28 people did evacuate to the one shelter opened on Guam, in an abundance of caution for those still recovering from Typhoon Mawar in 2023. They were able to return home the same day, following Bebinca���s passage.

Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No reports received of damage or impacts from the island of Rota or the CNMI Homeland Security Emergency Management office. The airport on Rota was closed so no observations were taken, but overall impacts support sustained winds less than 39 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saipan and Tinian reported no wind-related damage with the passage of Tropical Storm Bebinca. Near Saipan's airport (215 ft in elevation), max sustained wind of 31 kt (36 mph) was recorded around 0315ChST with a max gust of 38 kt (44 mph) around the same time. Only higher elevations saw winds around tropical storm strength with a private weather station on Wireless Ridge (elevation of 673 ft) reporting max sustained winds of 39 kt (45 mph) at 0325ChST and a max gust of 46 kt (53 mph) at 0655ChST. On Saipan, there were problems with runoff due to heavy showers, resulting in considerable erosion of gravel roads. Much of the highway/street infrastructure along western Saipan is under construction, with exposed gravel and dirt in many areas from San Antonio northward to Garapan. Problems were compounded in the days of monsoon surge later in the week, and more so on Monday, September 16 with a strong monsoon surge. Winds with the second surge exceeded those during Tropical Storm Bebinca, with more impacts on the 16th than the 10-11th. See High Wind entry for monsoon surge on September 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in the usual places during heavy rainfall as Tropical Storm Bebinca passed near Guam: Polaris Point/Route 1 in Sumay-Piti corridor and other common urban flood areas. Most of Guam saw 24 hour rainfall (2200ChST on September 10th to 2200ChST on September 11th) of between 5 and 7.50 inches, as reported by COOP, CoCoRaHS, USGS and ASOS stations across the island. According the automated gauges, most of the rain fell from 0200ChST to 1200ChST on September 11th. The weather office did not receive many reports of damaged or stalled vehicles due to flooded roads, so damage estimates were kept at 0 (zero) $USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Saipan, there were problems with runoff due to heavy showers as Tropical Storm Bebinca passed near Guam, resulting in considerable erosion of gravel roads. Much of the highway/street infrastructure along western Saipan is under construction, with exposed gravel and dirt in many areas from San Antonio northward to Garapan. Saipan International Airport recorded a 24 hour rainfall of 1.49 inches (2200ChST September 10th to 2200ChST September 11th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents within the western reefs of Guam led to dangerous swimming/wading conditions, leading to the death of a fisherman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guam Fire Department's water rescue team responded to Family Beach at 1700ChST when they received a reported of a overdue fisherman. Family Beach is located near the village of Piti, and is on a narrow strip of land that extends westward from Piti and the south side of the strip forms the northern boundary of Apra Harbor, while the northern side faces the Philippine Sea. Jointed by U.S. Coast Guard and Helicopter Sea Combat Squadron 25, crews searched until sunset Saturday and then resumed the search 0700ChST Sunday. At 0753ChST fire department rescue divers eventually located the body of the 24-year-old fisherman about 50 feet below the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ahead of Invest 95W, scattered showers started to develop across the Marianas with heavier showers across Guam due to weak winds allowing for island-enhanced showers to develop along the western side of Guam. The ASOS at the airport and near the WFO in Tiyan reported 0.70 inches of rain between 1200ChST and 1300ChST, with higher amounts likely near the western coastal villages of  Hag��t��a and Asan. Pictures from newspapers showed drivers navigate through a flooded section of Chalan Palasyo, commonly referred to as Snake Road and runs pass Navy Hospital, after heavy rains saturated the low lying valley area in Maina, a community near Asan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Man-yi (25W) originated from east of the Marshall islands where the Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) first designated it as Invest 93W, northeast of Majuro, on the 7th of November. This disturbance continued WNW, eventually becoming a tropical depression (25W) on the 9th of November, and later becoming a tropical storm later that day to the east of the Marianas. While Man-yi approached the Marianas, it was sheared by northwest winds aloft and heavy convection remained displaced to the south and southeast of the center. During the overnight hours of November 12th, the fast-moving low-level circulation center began turning WSW, then SW to eventually pass south of Guam pre-sunrise 13th Nov. With shear weakening, Man-yi began to intensify as it departed the Marianas. Man-yi would continue towards the Philippines and on November 16th JTWC upgraded Man-yi to super typhoon status with maximum sustained winds around 160 mph. Man-yi would make its first landfall in the Philippines near Panganiban, Catanduanes later that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Man-yi quickly passed south of Guam during the early morning hours of November 13th, making it closes approach to Guam between 0400ChST and 0500ChST at a distance around 40 miles SSE from the weather office in Tiyan. Maximum sustained winds of 30 knots (34 mph) and gusts of 42 knots (48 mph) were recorded by the ASOS at the airport and other stations across Guam. The minimum pressure recorded on Guam was 1005 mb, with the JTWC estimating the minimum central pressure was around 994 mb. Rainfall amounts were unimpressive across Guam as the weather office recorded a daily total of 0.09 inches and a two day total (Nov 12-13) of 0.40 inches, as most of the heavy showers were displaced south of the center due to strong wind shear (about 50 miles from Guam). The max water level measured by the Pago Bay Tide Gauge was 0.96 ft above MHHW. Impacts were minimal, mainly tree branches, some overturned canopies and downed political campaign signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Bebinca originated in the Federated States of Micronesia near Pohnpei and Kosrae as a surface trough. Bebinca gradually consolidated into a closed low-level circulation as it moved WNW past Pohnpei and Chuuk as was monitored as Invest 95W. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 14W as it sat about 200 miles SE of Guam on 10 Sep. It was briefly elevated to a 40 mph TS as it passed to the west of southern Guam early morning on the 11th. Rainfall was the more notable hazard with Guam seeing the bulk of rainfall. Heavy rains were less prevalent across the CNMI, but Saipan did see some erosion and runoff due to all the loose soil and gravel from large stretches of road work. Little wind damage was reported beyond some downed limbs and fleshier vegetations such as banana and papaya trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After the passage of TS Bebinca, a south-southwest monsoon pattern setup across the Marianas. An intense cluster of heavy showers and thunderstorms moved across Guam from Barrigada and up to Andersen Air Force Base (central to northern Guam) from about 1500ChST to 1600ChST on December 13th, just a couple of days after Bebinca developed just west of Guam and brought several inches of rain, so much of Guam was already saturated. The weather office recorded 2.4 inches within that one hour timeframe, with radar estimates of  2 to 4 inches across the area. Several reports of cars stalling on flooded roads with sections of the roads becoming impassable, such as the north and south bound outer lanes of  Route (Highway) 1 near Carlos Heights upper Tumon. Similar flooding was seen by a NWS employee on the low-lying section of Route (Highway) 8, which is located near the weather office. No official damage estimate was given, but a rough estimate of flood damage to stalled cars is estimated to be around 2.5 Thousand $USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: TS Jebi (19W) originated east of the CNMI as a weak circulation (JTWC Invest 95W) at the eastern end of a monsoon-like trough extending across the Philippine Sea around 25 Sep. Maintaining an asymmetric windfield and convective spread with strongest winds, showers and thunderstorms found east and south of the center, 95W lifted NNW, keeping east of Saipan. The Joint Typhoon Warning Center (JTWC) issued its first warning on TD 19W at 0100 ChST 27 Sep as it sat about 50 miles E of Alamagan Island. With JTWC Warning #3, issued at 1300ChST, JTWC classified 19W as a 40 mph tropical storm, centered 60 miles WNW of Pagan Island - which it had passed over a few hours prior. Residents reported strong winds and gusts with very heavy rainfall, but no wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With JTWC Warning #3, issued at 1300ChST on Sep 27th, JTWC classified 19W as a 40 mph tropical storm, centered 60 miles WNW of Pagan Island - which it had passed over a few hours prior. Residents reported strong winds and gusts with very heavy rainfall. The three far northern islands of the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) with people living on them during TS Jebi were Agrihan, Alamagan, and Pagan. Each island had 3 people living on them, making the total population affected 9  people. Due to the lack infrastructure on these islands, there are no weather reports for these islands. No reported impacts or damage from the 9 people living on the islands, nor from the Northern Mariana Islands mayor, who resides on Saipan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 1st, people on Jaluit Atoll reported damage due to a brief period of strong winds. Satellite imagery around the estimated time of damage, showed building cumulus and cumulonimbus near the atoll.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On September 1st, people on Jaluit Atoll, within the Marshall islands, reported damage due to a brief period of strong winds. Satellite imagery around the estimated time of damage, which was between 1200LST (1000ChST) and 1300LST (1100ChST), showed building cumulus and cumulonimbus near the atoll, suggesting the potential of a gust front or outflow boundary causing the damage. A Marshallese governmental PMU unit did an assessment and found 8 homes and 3 churches were damaged and initial reports suggest 43.3 Thousand $USD will be allocated to support the affected community. Photos taken of the damage showed tin roofs that were nailed to simple structures were either stripped off the roof or one side of the tin roof remained attached and the rest was rolled up by the wind. A few broadleaf (palm) trees were also shown to be blown down by the winds. Initial reports from Jaluit speculated that the damage was done by a tornado or waterspout. However, statements from the atoll do not mention a funnel or waterspout being reported, and some reports conflicting information. Some reported blues and then it suddenly became windy, while others said it was cloudy and raining when the strong winds occurred.  Since there are no firm statements of anyone claiming to have seen a funnel or waterspout, and damage reports cannot conclusively support a tornado or waterspout, this event is currently contributed to thunderstorms winds (outflow) though a tornado/waterspout cannot be ruled out completely.
